entries.devilDefinition: The rebranding of prompt fiddling once people realised the prompt had quietly become half the product.

entries.title: Context Poisoning
entries.devilDefinition: Sneaking bad instructions or bad facts into the system's memory and letting future competence be built on polluted paperwork.
entries.plainDefinition: Context poisoning is a failure mode or attack in which retrieved context, shared memory, cached notes, or prior state gets contaminated with false, hostile, or manipulative content that later steers model behaviour.

entries.plainDefinition: Context rot is the degradation that happens in long-running chats or agent workflows when accumulated instructions, stale assumptions, noisy history, and partial fixes start reducing output quality.

entries.title: Context Stuffing
entries.devilDefinition: Solving a thinking problem by feeding the model more text until latency, cost, and coherence all lodge a complaint.
entries.plainDefinition: Context stuffing is the habit of overloading a model with documents, instructions, examples, and chat history in the hope that sheer volume will compensate for poor retrieval or unclear task design.

entries.title: Context Window
entries.devilDefinition: The finite desk space on which the model must juggle your instructions, your documents, your tool outputs, and its own reply without knocking anything important onto the floor.
entries.plainDefinition: The context window is the maximum amount of tokenised input and generated output a model can consider in a single run. Everything the model can directly attend to during that interaction has to fit inside that limit.

entries.title: Distillation

entries.plainDefinition: Distillation is a training approach in which a smaller or cheaper model learns from the outputs, preferences, or intermediate signals of a larger model so it can reproduce some of its performance at lower cost.

entries.title: Eval-Driven Development
entries.devilDefinition: The radical idea that if your product depends on stochastic behaviour, you should probably measure something before calling the demo a platform.
entries.plainDefinition: Eval-driven development is the practice of using targeted evaluations to guide prompt changes, model selection, tool design, and release decisions for AI systems.

entries.plainDefinition: Evals are structured evaluations used to measure model or system performance against defined criteria. They may include benchmark tasks, human review, automated checks, red-team cases, regression tests, or task-specific business metrics.

entries.title: Inference

entries.title: Prompt
entries.devilDefinition: A politely worded attempt to steer a probability machine into behaving as if it has read the brief and cares.
entries.plainDefinition: A prompt is the input text, and sometimes structured context, provided to a model in order to shape its output. In LLM systems this can include instructions, examples, retrieved documents, tool results, formatting rules, and the user's actual request.

entries.title: Prompt Caching

entries.plainDefinition: Prompt caching is a technique where a provider reuses the already processed prefix of repeated prompts, reducing latency and input cost when the beginning of the request stays the same.

entries.title: Prompt debt
entries.devilDefinition: The future cost of every expedient line added to a prompt because shipping today felt nobler than understanding tomorrow.
entries.plainDefinition: Prompt debt is the accumulated maintenance burden, fragility, and hidden coupling created when prompts, examples, and guardrails are patched repeatedly without clear structure, tests, ownership, or retirement.

entries.devilDefinition: Hiding instructions in the tool layer so the agent mistakes a compromised capability for a helpful one.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool poisoning is an attack or failure mode in which a tool's descriptors, schemas, metadata, routing, or surrounding instructions are manipulated so an agent invokes it under false or malicious assumptions.

entries.devilDefinition: A menu of powers offered to an agent, ideally written by someone who understands the difference between capability and liability.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool registry is a structured catalogue of tools an AI system can discover or use, including each tool's name, schema, permissions, owner, description, and operational constraints.

entries.plainDefinition: Verifier model names the nearby AI idea that shows up when RLVR needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Vertex AI
entries.devilDefinition: Vertex AI is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Vertex AI is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.title: Vibe Coding
entries.devilDefinition: Programming by aesthetic momentum: if the demo works and the cursor keeps moving, architecture can file its complaint later.
entries.plainDefinition: Vibe coding is an informal style of software creation where a person steers AI coding tools with high-level intent, accepting rapid generated changes without necessarily understanding every implementation detail as they go.

entries.title: Vibe Engineer
entries.devilDefinition: A person who cannot always explain the system but can often make it produce something less embarrassing.
entries.plainDefinition: A semi-serious term for someone skilled at steering generative systems through prompts, examples, references, iteration, framing, and aesthetic judgement.

entries.title: vLLM
entries.devilDefinition: The serving engine invoked when a team wants more throughput, lower costs, and the right to sound unusually composed about GPU memory pressure.
entries.plainDefinition: vLLM is an open-source inference and serving engine for large models, designed to improve throughput and memory efficiency while exposing APIs for self-managed model serving.

entries.title: Voice Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A synthetic colleague hired to answer the phone with perfect stamina and the occasional inability to notice that a human is furious.
entries.plainDefinition: A voice agent is an AI system that can conduct spoken conversations, usually by combining speech recognition, language models, and text-to-speech in order to handle calls, bookings, support, or outbound interactions.

entries.title: Weights
entries.devilDefinition: The dense pile of numbers that turns "just an algorithm" into property, infrastructure, and geopolitical gossip.
entries.plainDefinition: Weights are the learned numerical parameters inside a model. They are what the model ends up using to make predictions and generate outputs after it has been built.

entries.title: Windsurf
entries.devilDefinition: An IDE for developers who would like to remain in flow state while the model edits the repo with the confidence of a colleague who has never been fired.
entries.plainDefinition: Windsurf is an AI coding editor centred on autocomplete, codebase-aware assistance, agentic task execution, rules, memory features, and integrated tooling inside the development environment.

entries.plainDefinition: Workflow software names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.

entries.title: Worktree
entries.devilDefinition: A separate checkout where the agent can make a mess without immediately decorating the main desk with it.
entries.plainDefinition: A worktree is a linked working tree attached to the same Git repository, allowing different branches or tasks to be checked out in separate directories at the same time.

entries.title: World Model
entries.devilDefinition: The flattering phrase for a system that appears to know enough structure for humans to start projecting philosophy onto it.
entries.plainDefinition: A world model is an internal representation that helps a system predict how states, objects, actions, or events relate to one another over time.

entries.title: xAI
entries.devilDefinition: A company name that sometimes arrives in conversation as though brand velocity were itself a benchmark.
entries.plainDefinition: xAI is a company that develops AI models and products, most notably the Grok family and associated consumer experiences. As with other vendors, the company name is distinct from the specific model or product being discussed.

entries.title: Yann LeCun
entries.devilDefinition: The prominent researcher most likely to answer AI melodrama with a technical correction and a shrug.
entries.plainDefinition: Yann LeCun is a Turing Award-winning researcher, formerly Meta's chief AI scientist, known for foundational work in deep learning and for public scepticism toward the more theatrical claims surrounding advanced AI.

entries.title: Zero Data Retention
entries.devilDefinition: The promise that your prompts may pass through the machine without joining its afterlife.
entries.plainDefinition: Zero data retention is a provider policy or product setting indicating that prompts and outputs are not stored for model improvement or long-term retention beyond limited operational handling.

entries.title: Zero-Shot
entries.devilDefinition: Asking the system to improvise on first contact and congratulating yourself when probability looks like generality.
entries.plainDefinition: Zero-shot refers to asking a model to perform a task without giving task-specific examples in the prompt.
